title: Introduction
text: You've already learned how the Louvain algorithm finds communities by optimizing modularity. Now, let's see it in action. We're going to project the fraud network, run Louvain, and witness how it can reduce your search space by an impressive 98%. [IMAGE: img-2-community-detection-fraud-3-reducing-search-space-s238-000-new] As you can see in this graph, the Louvain algorithm identifies tightly-knit groups, effectively shrinking the search space by highlighting clusters of nodes that represent communities.

title: What You'll Learn
text: By the end of this lesson, you'll be equipped to: create a heterogeneous graph projection for fraud analysis, run Louvain in both stats and write modes, identify communities that contain known fraudsters, and focus your investigation on high-priority communities.

title: Step 1: Project the Graph
text: Let's start by creating a projection of Users, Cards, and Devices. Here's the Cypher code you'll use to create the fraud graph projection. This code matches users to their cards and devices, capturing the relationships that are crucial for detecting fraud. The projection is built by considering both directed and undirected relationships, which is key to understanding the network's structure.

title: Understanding the Projection
text: This projection involves some important decisions. Why include Cards and Devices? Well, fraudsters often share infrastructure, so including these nodes allows Louvain to find communities based on shared cards and devices, not just direct transactions. [IMAGE: img-2-community-detection-fraud-3-reducing-search-space-s241-002-new] As shown in this flowchart, the decision to make 'HAS_CC' and 'USED' undirected is because sharing is bidirectional. If User A and User B both have the same card, that connection is mutual. Keeping 'P2P' directed is crucial because the direction of transactions matters; money flows from sender to receiver, and this asymmetry can reveal fraud patterns.

title: Step 2: Run Louvain in Stats Mode
text: Before we write any results, let's preview what Louvain will uncover. Use this Cypher code to call Louvain in stats mode. This will yield the community count, distribution, and modularity, giving us a sneak peek into the structure Louvain identifies.

title: Interpreting Stats Results
text: You should see approximately 11,500 communities and a modularity score of about 0.98. Remember from Lesson 2, a modularity score above 0.4 indicates a useful structure. A score of 0.98 means the communities are extremely well-defined, with nodes within communities being far more connected to each other than to outsiders. The community distribution will show size statistics, including min, max, mean, and percentiles.

title: Step 3: Run Louvain in Write Mode
text: Now, let's write the community IDs back to the database. Use this Cypher code to call Louvain in write mode. This will assign a 'louvainCommunityId' to each node, indicating which community it belongs to, and return the community count and modularity.

title: What Just Happened?
text: Louvain has analyzed the projected nodes and discovered natural groupings. Each node now has a 'louvainCommunityId' property, showing which community it belongs to. [IMAGE: img-2-community-detection-fraud-3-reducing-search-space-s245-004-new] This illustration shows nodes grouped into communities, with labels indicating community IDs and highlighting dense intra-community connections. Nodes in the same community are more densely connected to each other than to the rest of the network.

title: A Note on Community IDs
text: Your community IDs will differ from any examples shown. Louvain is non-deterministic, meaning the specific IDs assigned depend on the processing order. What truly matters is the grouping, not the ID numbers. Always use the IDs from your results when following along.

title: Step 4: Visualize Communities
text: Let's visualize the community structure. Use this Cypher code to match paths where users share the same community ID. This will allow you to see how users cluster around shared cards and devices, which is exactly the kind of infrastructure sharing we're aiming to detect.

title: Step 5: Count Fraudulent Communities
text: Now, let's determine how many communities contain known fraudsters. This Cypher code will count communities with and without fraud, giving you a clear picture of the landscape.

title: The Power of Community Detection
text: You should find approximately 200 communities with at least one flagged fraudster and about 11,500 communities with no flagged fraudsters. That's roughly 1.7% of communities containing known fraud. Louvain has just reduced your search space by a staggering 98%.

title: Why This Matters
text: Before using Louvain, you had 204,000 users to investigate. After Louvain, you're down to about 200 communities worth examining. [IMAGE: img-2-community-detection-fraud-3-reducing-search-space-s250-006-new] This before-and-after comparison highlights the efficiency gained through Louvain, allowing you to deprioritize the vast majority of users and focus on the suspicious minority.

title: Step 6: Rank Communities by Fraud
text: Not all fraudulent communities are equal. Let's find the most suspicious ones. This Cypher code will rank communities by fraud indicators, helping you identify which ones deserve the most attention.

title: Interpreting the Rankings
text: The results will show the Louvain community ID, total users in that community, known fraudsters, and the percentage of the community that's flagged. A high flagged count indicates more known fraud, while a high flagged percentage suggests more concentrated fraud. Note the community ID at the top of your results, as you'll investigate it in the next steps. Remember, your ID will differ from others'.

title: Step 7: Set a Parameter for Investigation
text: Pick the top community from your results and set it as a parameter. Use this Cypher code to set your community ID, replacing the example ID with the one from your results. This step is crucial for focusing your investigation on the most suspicious community.

title: Step 8: Examine the Community
text: Now, let's see the breakdown of flagged versus unflagged users within your chosen community. This Cypher code will count the flagged and unflagged users, helping you identify potential investigation targets.

title: What This Tells Us
text: You should see two rows: users with 'fraudMoneyTransfer = 0' (unflagged) and users with 'fraudMoneyTransfer = 1' (flagged). The unflagged users are our investigation targets—they're in a fraud-heavy community but haven't been identified yet. Are they accomplices, victims, or mules? That's what we need to find out.

title: Step 9: Visualize the Community
text: Let's visualize how users in this community connect. Use this Cypher code to see the connections within the community. [IMAGE: img-2-community-detection-fraud-3-reducing-search-space-s256-008-new] This network diagram highlights flagged and unflagged users, as well as shared infrastructure like cards or devices. Look for flagged users clustered together, unflagged users connected to multiple flagged users, and shared cards or devices linking suspicious accounts. These patterns suggest which unflagged users deserve closer scrutiny.

title: What We've Achieved
text: Let's recap our progress. We started with about 790,000 nodes and 204,000 users. After applying Louvain, we're down to around 200 suspicious communities. Finally, we've focused on a few hundred users within a specific community. This transformation has turned an impossible manual task into a focused investigation.

title: The Remaining Question
text: We've identified communities containing fraud, but within each community, which users are most suspicious? Who should we investigate first? How do we prioritize hundreds of potential suspects? These are the questions we'll tackle next.

title: What's Next
text: In Lesson 4, you'll learn two algorithms that help with formal community assignment: Degree Centrality, which identifies high-connection nodes that could be potential hubs or noise, and Weakly Connected Components (WCC), which offers deterministic community assignment for auditable results. These tools will help you move from exploration to actionable suspect lists.

title: Cleanup
text: Finally, let's drop the projection. Use this Cypher code to call the graph drop function. You can keep the projection if you want to experiment further, but the 'louvainCommunityId' property is already written to nodes, so the projection is no longer needed for the analysis we've done.

title: Summary
text: In this lesson, you've used Louvain to dramatically reduce your search space. You've created a heterogeneous projection capturing users and shared infrastructure, found approximately 11,500 communities with a modularity of 0.98, identified around 200 communities (1.7%) containing known fraud, and focused on the most fraudulent community for investigation. Louvain has transformed an impossible 204,000-user investigation into a manageable set of suspicious communities.
